title: Hierarchical crack patterns: Identification of crack generations
authors: Yuri Yu. Tarasevich, Andrei S. Burmistrov, Andrei V. Eserkepov
abstract: Hierarchical crack patterns of various origins are ubiquitous in the world around us. We reduce the problem of classifying crack generations in an image of a part of the entire hierarchical crack pattern to the well-known topological sorting of a directed acyclic graph. The classification demonstrates robustness to reasonable shifts in the pattern image boundaries.
summary: This paper proposes a robust graph-theoretic method to classify the generation sequence of cracks in hierarchical crack patterns. It models the pattern as a DAG and uses a leaf-removal algorithm, which successfully reduces the classification problem to topological sorting. The resulting method is shown to be highly stable against minor changes in the analyzed image boundaries.
main_problem: The paper addresses the problem of classifying the generation order of cracks within a Hierarchical Crack Pattern (HCP) image fragment.
main_result: The proposed method successfully reduces the classification to topological sorting, yielding a robust generation assignment that is stable even when the image boundaries shift.
method: The core method involves defining cracks as chains of edges near 180 degrees and constructing a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) where arcs point from child to parent at T-junctions. Generation is then determined by iteratively removing leaves (vertices with zero in-degree) until all vertices are processed.
model_or_system: The physical system is the Hierarchical Crack Pattern (HCP) found in various materials, which is digitized and modeled as a graph structure. The analysis assumes the graph is connected, has vertex degrees $\le 3$, and contains no loops.
key_observables: Crack generations, represented by the topological ordering of the DAG vertices.
important_parameters: The acceptable angular deviation for defining a crack ($\le 30^\circ$).
main_assumptions: The network must be strictly hierarchical, allowing for a clear child-parent relationship, and the graph must satisfy degree $\le 3$ and no loops.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the construction of the graph and DAG, showing the step-by-step processing of the DAG using the proposed leaf-removal algorithm, and comparing results against older methods under boundary shifts.
paper_structure: The paper first introduces the problem and the concept of crack definition. It then details the DAG construction and the leaf-removal algorithm for topological sorting. Finally, it presents results demonstrating the method's robustness compared to existing techniques.
why_interesting: While the topic is materials science/image analysis, the methodology—reducing a complex structural classification problem to a graph-theoretic sorting problem—shares conceptual parallels with analyzing connectivity or structure in complex quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
